text: Coiled sleeve, conversion to Roux-en-Y gastric bypass. Patient is a fifty-year-old female with history of hypertension and obstructive sleep apnea. She underwent a laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy two years ago. She did well after the surgery, tolerating diet normally. Six months ago, she began to experience dysphagia to solids, tolerating only some liquids with constant daily vomiting. Her BMI before sleeve was fifty-four. Her actual BMI prior to the conversion is nineteen.

The upper GI fluoroscopy showed evident passage of contrast with two acute kinks on the sleeve, one superior and another inferior, with a proximal dilatation, as it can be clearly seen in the following images. The esophagogastroduodenoscopy showed a large proximal gastric dilatation with a first acute angulation that could be easily transversed. At the level of the incisora, another acute angulation did not allow the passage of the scope despite several attempts.

Trocars are placed in the abdominal wall for laparoscopic access. The surgeon performs a diagnostic laparoscopy, revealing two angulations in the gastric tube with adhesions. Using a monopolar hook, the surgeon begins dissection at the hiatus, lysing adhesions to free the gastric tube.

The surgeon mobilizes the stomach and dissects a posterior window around the gastric tube. Using two stapler loads, the stomach is transected. The transverse colon is retracted to identify the angle of Treitz. The jejunum is measured fifty centimeters from the angle of Treitz and transected with a stapler. The alimentary limb is measured to be one hundred and twenty centimeters, and enterotomies are performed using a monopolar hook.

The surgeon performs a side-to-side anastomosis using a stapler, followed by oversewing the enterotomies in two layers with 3-0 absorbable sutures. The gastrojejunostomy begins with a continuous posterior outer layer of sutures, followed by a continuous inner-posterior layer. After completing the posterior layer, an inner-anterior layer is started, with sutures joining from opposite sides in the middle. The outer anterior layer is completed to finalize the anastomosis.
The OR time was a hundred and twenty minutes. The estimated blood loss, ten cc's. No complications were registered and discharge was post-op day number one.
